title: podman unshare does not work on standard user account added to coreos on pi4.
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I am unable to run podman unshare.  I have su'd to a standard user account from the core user of coreos on a pi4b.  I get the following errors when running the podman unshare command.
```Failed to connect to bus: Operation not permitted (consider using --machine=<user>@.host --user to connect to bus of other user)```
I shown a root prompt, and I run ```podman image mount "container_name"```:
```ERRO[0000] stat /run/user/0: no such file or directory```
my standard user account had a similar error, and on asking the coreos matrix, I was told to linger the user, which I did, and this got me a /run/user/uid directory.   I am inclined to suspect that this is a coreos configuration issue.
I have tried using systemd-run and the other suggestions from the troubleshooting advice, but to no avail.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.  login to coreos core user by using ssh

2. su into standard user account

3. run podman unshare

**Describe the results you received:**
error msg - ```Failed to connect to bus: Operation not permitted (consider using --machine=<user>@.host --user to connect to bus of other user)```
+ root prompt.   run podman image mount container_name or any other podman command and get error ```ERRO[0000] stat /run/user/0: no such file or directory``` 
 
**Describe the results you expected:**
functional uid root prompt 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
I am on coreos, on pi4b and it may not be configured correctly, although it is the standard configuration.  I couldn't get a systemd session using the user with /run/user/uid not existing, until I lingered the user using loginctl.  I think this may be coreos misconfigured.
Also, to get a systemd session working in the standard user account I had to append the following lines to the standard user's .bashrc:
```
export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR="/run/user/$UID"
export DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS="unix:path=${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/bus"
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.4.4
API Version:  3.4.4
Go Version:   go1.16.8
Built:        Wed Dec  8 21:48:10 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/arm64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

  kernel: 5.16.13-200.fc35.aarch64

    rootless: true
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
coreos - rpm-ostree so no.
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
latest coreos on pi4b

comments.body:
Hi @millerthegorilla , I think this is because somehow the systemd session is not created properly.

Could you share output of `echo $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR $UID` please ?
I think you would either need to do `ssh` into the user which are using or create a valid session using `machinectl login`.

I'm not at all sure but I don't think this is a podman issue looks more like issue with `systemd` configuration issue.

Inside podman unshare $UID will always be 0, thus your `export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR="/run/user/$UID"` in bashrc will point to the wrong location.

Using su is not recommended, even if you enable lingering you still have to at least use `su --login` to set the proper env vars.
It is best to use `machinectl shell -q username@` to change the user with systemd or setup ssh for localhost.

If you want to use your `export` hack in .bashrc you should check for the `_CONTAINERS_ROOTLESS_UID` env var and use this when defined.

@Luap99   I did as you said, removing the 'hack' from .bashrc, and used ```sudo machinectl shell -q username@``` to login.  It logged in ok, but when I ran podman unshare, I got the following error.
```
Failed to connect to bus: Operation not permitted (consider using --machine=<user>@.host --user to connect to bus of other user)
```
I did manage to get the podman unshare command to work, but that was when the 'hack' was in .bashrc, and I su'd into the user account, then commented out the 'hack'.  I was then able to run the podman unshare command.
I looked for the env var _CONTAINERS_ROOTLESS_UID but it was not defined.

With that in mind, and because I need to be able to run commands in the standard user account from scripts that are based in the core account, I used the following different 'hack' in the standard user's .bashrc to get the podman unshare command working.
```bash
if [[ ${UID} -eq 1001 ]]
then
    export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR="/run/user/$UID"
    export DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS="unix:path=${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/bus"
fi
```
machinectl should set the correct `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` and `DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS` values, what are the values when you run it without the export in your bashrc?
does it make any difference if this user is lingering?
